coredata.dc:title: A new class of electromagnetic shields based on carbon dots adorning Te nanorods embedded into PEDOT:PSS for protection from electromagnetic (EM) pollutions
coredata.dc:creator: Al Naim, Abdullah F.
Ibrahim, Sobhy S.
El-Shamy, Ahmed G.
coredata.dc:description: Environmental pollutions, especially those caused by the electromagnetic radiations, are considered one of the most challenging issues facing today's world. Therefore, shields based on polymeric nanocomposites have had much concern nowadays. In this regard, a new class of conducting films based on Carbon dots (Cdots) adorned Telluride (Te) Nanorods incorporated into Poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):Poly(styrenesulphonate) (CTe/PEDOT:PSS) nanocomposite has been fabricated for the shielding applications in the frequency regime 8–12 GHz. The impact of synergistic effect of Cdots and Te on surface morphology, electrical and electromagnetic interference features has been studied according to the matrix (C x Te10-x ), where x is the Cdots concentration. A brilliant distribution, high dispersion, good adhesion features and excellent compatibility of CTe nanoparticles have been obtained in the PEDOT:PSS according to SEM. At x = 8 wt%, the nanocomposite CTe/PEDOT:PSS has recorded a high in plane σ// = 237.87 ± 5 S/cm owing to the high packing of PEDOT nano-crystallites in the shield and a high out plane σ⊥ = 200.52 ± 4 S/cm thanks to the increase in the carrier mobility and the formation of CTe conductive paths. The SET ~ 63.17 dB has been achieved at 8 GHz for x = 8 wt% thanks to the high σ and the synergistic effect of Cdots and Te. The higher SET value of the obtained films makes these composites compete the higher class of shields especially with the use of tiny amount of C8Te2 nanoparticles. The absorption is the main shielding effectiveness mechanism in this composite. Finally, this study introduces a new, conductive, and an excellent EM wave absorber nanocomposite based on PEDOT:SS, Cdots and Te nano-rods. It has been predicted that the CTe/PEDOT:PSS shields have a prospective applications in biological, microwave shielding, electronic and wearable apparatuses at an affordable cost.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Full XPS spectra of the CTe nanoparticles shows three spectra C, O and Te elements, Inset of Figure, shows the ratio of elements. (b) High resolution XPS spectra of the carbon (C1s). (c) High resolution XPS spectra of the oxygen (O1s). (d) High resolution XPS spectra of the Te.

(a), TEM image of Te nano-rods, inset of Figure, histogram shows the average diameter of the Te nano-rods = 50 ± 4 nm. (b) HRTEM image of CTe nanoparticles show the decoration and distribution of Cdots on the surface of Te nano-rods, with the (110) plane of CDots with a lattice space 0.22 nm and plane (011) of Te nanoparticles with a lattice space 0.33 nm. (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the CTe nanoparticles, shows the isotherm type IV with a hysteresis loop (H3-type). (d) BJH measurements for the corresponding pore size distribution of CTe nanoparticles, shows the surface area, pore size and pore volume and they were found to be 80.51 m2g−1, 1.8 nm and 0.191 cm3g−1. This structure has a benefit in facilitating the adsorption of PEDOT:PSS on the CTe surface, improving the conducting paths that enhance the σ, improving the EM absorption and hence increasing the SET value.

shows the XPS spectra of the Sulphur S(2p) of the Te/PEDOT:PSS films, and CTe/PEDOT:PSS films. As seen the decrease in the intensity of S(2p) spectrum of PSS of the CTe/PEDOT:PSS films and its shift to lower energy proving that the strong π-π interaction of Cdots (via their functionalized groups) with the PEDOT:PSS polymer (via PSS chains), proving the role of Cdots playing it in improving the carrier transport and facilitates the charge transfer channels by reducing the carrier jump barrier, hopping, and producing a higher σ value.

shows the FESEM micrographs of the achieved shield films (a) the Te/PEDOT:PSS films (x = 0 wt%). (b) CTe/PEDOT:PSS films (x = 2 wt%) where the Cdots adorned on the Te surface inside the matrix. (c) CTe/PEDOT:PSS films (x = 4 wt%) where a large amount of CTe embedded into the matrix. (d) CTe/PEDOT:PSS films (x = 6 wt%) where a great amount of CTe embedded into the matrix, due to the strong H-bonding and strong π-π interactions between Cdots and PEDOT:PSS (e) CTe/PEDOT:PSS films (x = 8 wt%) where a good compbitabity and well dispersion of CTe inside the matrix with a high Cdots content which enables the sink and embedding the CTe in the films, (f) Cdots/PEDOT:PSS films (x = 10 wt%) where all the particles are embedding into the matrix thanks to the strong H-bonding and strong π-π interactions between Cdots and the PEDOT:PSS.

(a) shows the in plane electrical conductivity (σ//) and (through) out plane electrical conductivity (σ⊥) of the CTe/PEDOT:PSS films as a function of the (X = 0–10 wt%) content. (b) The carrier mobility (μ) of the CTe/PEDOT:PSS films as a function of the X concentration (x = 0–10 wt%).

(a) The total EM shielding effectiveness (SET) values of CTe/PEDOT:PSS films with different x concentration (0–10 wt%) as a function of frequency. (b) Scheme shows the mechanism of the transmission, internal reflection and absorption of electromagnetic wave with the nanocomposite films. Also, the scheme shows all the physical phenomena participating in the shielding mechanism such as (interfacial polarization, conduction loss, internal scattering, Capacitor-like phenomena and others). (c) The absorption, transmission and reflection coefficients of the CTe/PEDOT:PSS shields as a function on x content.

(a) The SEA values of CTe/PEDOT:PSS films with different x concentration (0–10 wt%) as a function of frequency (b) The SEA of CTe/PEDOT:PSS films as a function of the through/out plane conductivity (σ⊥). (c) The SER values of CTe/PEDOT:PSS films with different x concentration (0–1 wt%) as a function of frequency (d) The SER of CTe/PEDOT:PSS films as a function of the in plane conductivity (σ//).

(a) The Real dielectric constant (ε') values of CTe/PEDOT:PSS films with different x concentration (0–10 wt%) as a function of frequency (b) The imaginary dielectric constant (dielectric lossε ' ') values of CTe/PEDOT:PSS films with different x concentration (0–10 wt%) as a function of frequency.

illustrates the preparation Scenario of (a) Cdots nanoparticles, (b) Te nano-rods and CTe nanoparticles and (c) CTe/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposite films. (d) Camera picture shows the casting films, free standing film and the film flexibility. (e) The possible interactions produce inside the CTe/PEDOT:PSS films such as (i) Ionic interactions between PSS and PEDOT. (ii) Hydrogen bonding between the function groups (-COOH, -OH) and PSS units. (iii) Van der Waals interaction produces between Te atom layer and π interaction from the PEDOT:PSS chains at the polymer/nano-crystals interface results in a good interfacial adhesion between the two phases. (iv) π-π interactions interaction produces between CTe (via Cdots) and PEDOT:PSS polymers.
EMI shielding properties of the reported based shielding materials in X-band frequency.

head.ce:title: A new class of electromagnetic shields based on carbon dots adorning Te nanorods embedded into PEDOT:PSS for protection from electromagnetic (EM) pollutions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental pollutions, especially those caused by the electromagnetic radiations, are considered one of the most challenging issues facing today's world. Therefore, shields based on polymeric nanocomposites have had much concern nowadays. In this regard, a new class of conducting films based on Carbon dots (Cdots) adorned Telluride (Te) Nanorods incorporated into Poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):Poly(styrenesulphonate) (CTe/PEDOT:PSS) nanocomposite has been fabricated for the shielding applications in the frequency regime 8–12 GHz. The impact of synergistic effect of Cdots and Te on surface morphology, electrical and electromagnetic interference features has been studied according to the matrix (C x Te10-x ), where x is the Cdots concentration. A brilliant distribution, high dispersion, good adhesion features and excellent compatibility of CTe nanoparticles have been obtained in the PEDOT:PSS according to SEM. At x = 8 wt%, the nanocomposite CTe/PEDOT:PSS has recorded a high in plane σ// = 237.87 ± 5 S/cm owing to the high packing of PEDOT nano-crystallites in the shield and a high out plane σ⊥ = 200.52 ± 4 S/cm thanks to the increase in the carrier mobility and the formation of CTe conductive paths. The SET ~ 63.17 dB has been achieved at 8 GHz for x = 8 wt% thanks to the high σ and the synergistic effect of Cdots and Te. The higher SET value of the obtained films makes these composites compete the higher class of shields especially with the use of tiny amount of C8Te2 nanoparticles. The absorption is the main shielding effectiveness mechanism in this composite. Finally, this study introduces a new, conductive, and an excellent EM wave absorber nanocomposite based on PEDOT:SS, Cdots and Te nano-rods. It has been predicted that the CTe/PEDOT:PSS shields have a prospective applications in biological, microwave shielding, electronic and wearable apparatuses at an affordable cost.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollutions, especially caused by the electromagnetic (EM) radiations, are considered one of the most challenging issues facing today's world. This is because of their dangerous and harmful side effects on the human health. The prolonged exposure to electromagnetic radiations leads to the damage of DNA strands, brain tumor, cancer, and etc., [1]. In fact, the wireless apparatus and electronic devices are considered the main sources for emitting these electromagnetic radiations/pollutions. So, with increasing the military racing and the progress of technology, the inhibiting, blocking and the shielding from the electromagnetic radiations became an essential requirement [2]. The protection from electromagnetic radiations, especially the microwave frequencies, becomes one of the most important scientific topics attracting the attention of scientists and researchers, and measuring the assurance and quality of life in modern cultures in the current era [3]. The electromagnetic shielding is obtained by inhibiting, reducing and/or blocking (i) the magnetic field by using magnetic agent and/or (ii) electrical fields by using electric agent, from diffusion (passage) to another area via the wave reflected, absorbed and/or dissipated the radiation power in the material [4]. In spite of the great electrical conductivities and shielding features of the metals and alloys materials which were firstly used for a long time in this regard, great issues have been recorded for these metals such as being instable (environmental corrosion), mechanically inflexible, expensive, heavy and so forth [5]. Moreover, EM wave pollutions are not totally vanished or blocked where at the surface of the metal shield; these waves are reflected to the environment (outside the shield) which is considered another great issue. Therefore, it is vital to improve new materials to overcome these above-mentioned problems [6].

Therefore, new features, such as high environmental/corrosion resistance, lightness, resilience, affordability, and productability, appeared with increasing the progress of technology (electronic and wireless industries) [7]. Beside these features, shields should have a large EM absorption merit to block the electromagnetic waves to fulfill the requests of military, electric and medical technologies [8]. Thus, polymeric nanocomposites, based on the absorption of EM waves, have appeared with lightweight feature, flexibility and good shielding functions as a response for these desires [9]. In fact, most polymers used in the shielding are insulators which allow the waves to pass without any loss that is considered a great issue [1]. Therefore, different metals and/or carbon materials are incorporated into these polymeric materials as fillers to improve its shielding performance. No limited numbers of conducting grade nanofillers are incorporated into these insulating polymeric materials to enhance/improve the absorption of the EM radiation and attenuate the EM power in order to overcome the aforementioned limitations. This enhancement in the absorption of the EM radiation and attenuation the EM power was achieved by radiation power dissipation in the conducting nano-dopants and blocking/inhibiting the total reflection occurring at the polymeric surfaces [2,3]. Very recently, the intrinsic conducting polymers have been extensively applied in the EMI shielding works as a conducting functional component for obtaining greatly bendable and productive EM shields [10–13]. For example, the PEDOT:PSS has been mixed with waterborne polyurethane (WPU) for obtaining σ = 77 × 102 S/m and a great SET = 62 dB at 8–12 GHz [14]. As well, 100 μL Ti3C2Tx/PEDOT:PSS has been constructed to obtain a SET = 41 dB with a σ = 299 × 102 ± 2 S/m at 8–12 GHz [15]. The structure of (Graphite nano-platelets/PEDOT:PSS) has been designed for EMI shielding application. It was found that 25 wt% of Graphite nano-platelet into PEDOT:PSS gave the σ = 684 ± 0.06 S/m and SET value 70 dB at 8–12 GHz [16]. Moreover, Mxene (83.3 wt%) has been incorporated into PEDOT:PSS to obtain σ = 183.24 × 102 S/m and SET = 28.2 dB at 8–12 GHz [17]. Also the structure (PEDOT:PSS/Mxene 70 wt%) has been treated with H2SO4 acid to obtain a σ = 67.52 × 102 S/m and SET = 40.5 dB at 8–12 GHz [18]. Visibly, recent studies prove that the increase in σ leads to growing the total SET; however, constructing polymeric networks with a great σ is not an ideal solution to manufacture perfect shields. The increase in σ produces high EM reflections as a result of the impedance mismatches among the shields and the space wave [19]. Generally, these conductive shields with their homogeneous networks show large SER > 90%, regardless of their high SET, which produce severe secondary EM pollution and complicate the EM environs [13]. More advances, engineering of (i) 3D conductive networks polymeric composites and (ii) multiple interfaces conductive polymers (e.g. multi-component structures, multilayered/sandwiched, segregated, and others [20,21]) have shown high EMI shielding functions. For example, 35 μm of hollow and solid glass microspheres (40 vol% HGM and 30 vol% SGM) were inserted into the structure (polydimethylsiloxane PDMS/MWCNT 3 vol%) to obtain a SET = 52.7 and 55.2 dB, respectively, at 8–12 GHz [22]. Then, 50 vol% thermo-expandable microspheres were inserted into the un-foamed (PDMS/MWCNT) to obtain multiple interface structure for attaining σ ~ 70 S/cm and SET = 43 dB [23]. Although the promising EMI functions of these materials, however they express huge issues in (1) their harsh preparation conditions, e.g. great pressure, temperature, hard processability, high cost, usage of toxic ingredients, and also (2) liability to the damage of 3D conductive networks when removing the used template. (3) Some of the multiple interface conducting materials have weak EMI shielding functions. (4) The weak interfacial interactions between the components which deteriorate the mechanical features. (5) Difficult, complex, and hard fabrication processing of these materials which hinder its industrial large-scale productions. (6) The difficulty to design and engineer a high absorption coefficient material based on EMI shielding multiple interfaces conducting polymer to overcome the second EM wave pollutions, all of these problems lead to hindering the large-scale productions of these materials [20,21]. Additionally, the badly dispersion of nanofillers in the polymeric matrix, selection the suitable type of fillers, poor durability for achieving conductive polymeric nanocomposite, the higher content of the fillers, the processes of agglomeration and aggregation caused by the poor compatibility and weak interfacial interactions are still challenging problems for these materials which weaken their properties and inhibit their large applications [24].

Based on the above-mentioned benefits and limitations, one can say great EM absorptions and relative small EM reflections are the main desires for obtaining the perfect shield for the advanced electrical apparatus and next-generation of high-energy technology [1,13]. Thus the author expects that the combination between the polymeric, quasi-metal nanoparticles and carbonic material will produce new polymeric nanocomposites with high shielding performance and avoid the drawbacks. Therefore, we introduce in this literature a new EM shield based on telluride Te nano-rods adorned with the carbon dots Cdots inserted into the PEDOT:PSS polymers (CTe/PEDOT:PSS) with a high bendable, tiny filler content, high conductivity and EMI shielding functions. A marketable poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene):poly (styrenesulphonate) (PEDOT:PSS) polymers are broadly used, among all their counterparts, in various applications, such as thermoelectrics, wearable devices and others, owing to its bendability, affordability, durability, and its large conductivity [25,26]. However, when PEDOT:PSS was applied alone in EM shielding it was found that SET < 20 dB which is not sufficient to satisfy the requirements and considered great issues that hinder its applications in EM shielding/protection [27]. Also, this PEDOT:PSS polymer has shortcomings in term of (i) the redox states of PEDOT are instable and (ii) the oxidization levels of PEDOT are very difficult to be adjusted which are considered great issues that hinder its applications [25–28]. On the other hand, the telluride (Te) ranks as one of the upmost important quasi-metallic particles especially in their nano-scales [29]. Te nanoparticles are applied in many practical applications as they are simply fabricated in aqueous medium in the existence of directing surfactant agents [30]. However, Te is still available for the EMI shielding application until now and we attribute that to its low conductivity, which limits its large scale production [31]. Alternatively, the carbon dots have been recently classified as one of the upmost carbon materials [32,33]. Today, the carbon dots have been applied in electromagnetic shielding technologies due to their high electrical conductivities and their surface functionalized groups [1–3]. Also, recently, they have been inserted into many applications such as photocatalysis, adsorption, and thermoelectrics [32] due to its non-toxicity, brilliant luminescence, excellent performance, great water solubility, photostability, bio-compatibility, energy conversion and other distinctive features [33].

PEDOT:PSS (CLEVIOS PH1000) with a 1.3 wt% concentration, l-ascorbic acid (99.9%) and Na2TeO3 (99.9%) were delivered from the Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals. d-Glucose (99.9%), and Ethanol (99.9%) were delivered from Al-Naser Co., Egypt.

Cdots and Te rods in nano-sizes have been produced via the microwave approach and the chemical reduction method, respectively [34–39], See Section S1 and S2. The adornment of Cdots on the nano-Te rods has been performed by the precipitation method to obtain the (CTe) nano-particles [40], See Section S3.

The casting approach has been used to obtain the films of CTe/PEDOT:PSS nano-composite (Scheme 1) [41–45]. The fabrication of films was performed as follow: CxTe10-x nanofillers with various (x = 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 wt%) concentrations, have been slowly dropped with the rate (drop/s) to an aqueous PEDOT:PSS at R.T. The produced mixtures were left under a mechanical stirring for 60 min to obtain a homogenous solution. Then, the produced mixtures were casted and transferred to an oven at 60 °C for 6 h. The film thickness (50 ± 3 μm) is determined by a digital micrometer.

A (AXIS ULTRA, Kratos Analytical) XPS apparatus was conducted to detect the elements in the obtained particles. TEM microscope (JEOL-JEM-1230) was applied to prove the adornment of Cdot on the nano-Te rods and determine the size of the nanoparticles. A (Micromeritics ASAP 2020, USA) BET instrument was conducted to examine the BET surface area, pore size and pore volume of the CTe particles in a liquid N2 environs. The morphology of the film surface has been examined by (JEOL JSM- 6500F) FESEM. By applying AC magnetic field on the van der Pauw configuration, the Hall parameters were measured. A Keithley 6517B electrometer was conducted to measure the conductivities σ of the obtained films. The measuring of the through plane σ⊥ and in plane σ// was done by using the two-probe and four-probe methods, respectively. The measuring uncertainties of σ⊥ and σ// were ≥5%. A (Hewlett–Packard with a spectroanalyzer) waveguide approach was conducted to obtain the EM shielding features of the shield with dimensions (3 cm × 3 cm × 50 ± 3 μm) in the 8–12 GHz band according to ASTM D5568-08.

Full XPS spectra have been scanned for the CTe nanoparticles to identify the element compositions (Fig. 1 ). As seen, four spectra were identified at binding energy 287.3 and 532.7 eV for the C and O elements in Cdots nanoparticles and 576.6 and 587.6 eV for Te nano-rods (Fig. 1a). Fig. 1b shows the high resolution of C1s spectrum. As seen, four spectra were identified at binding energies 284.7 eV for the C–OH bond, 287.3 eV for the COOH bond, 285.9 eV for CO or CO (sp2 hybridization of carbon), and the CC (sp2 hybridization of carbon) at 283.8 eV [46]. On the other hand, the high resolution of O1s spectrum has been scanned (Fig. 1c). As seen, three spectra were identified at binding energies 533.3 eV for the O=C–OH and/or C–OH, 532.3 eV for the CO and 531.2 eV for the CO [47]. Also, two spectra have been identified from the high resolution of Te spectrum at binding energies 573.6 eV for Te 3d5/2 and 583.9 eV for Te 3d3/2 for Te nano-rods [48] (Fig. 1d). In fact, these function groups (-OH, COOH, -COO and others) facilitate (i) the interactions among CTe and polymeric chains which are reflected in improving the conductivity, and (ii) enables the interaction between CTe and the incident EM waves which increases the EM absorption and hence improving the SEA value.

More confirmation was done by performing the TEM measurements (Fig. 2a,b). Fig. S1 displays the TEM of Cdots which appears in a spherical and uniform shape with their mean diameters ~5.4 ± 1 nm. Fig. 2a shows the TEM images for Te nano-rods. As seen, the nano-Te rods have a mean diameter 50 ± 4 nm. They are allied in a parallel manner with high density and they are uniformly distributed with sharp and regular edges. Moreover, large-quantities of the nano-Te rods are produced. Fig. 2b displays the TEM of CTe particles. As shown, the Cdot particles are adorned on the Te surface. The TEM image shows the (110) plane of CDots with a lattice space 0.22 nm and plane (011) of Te fillers with a lattice space 0.33 nm.

BET experiments were carried out to evaluate the adsorption-desorption feature and specific surface area of the obtained nanoparticles (Fig. 2c,d). As detected, the particle shows the isotherm type IV with a hysteresis loop (H3-type) [49] (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d presents the pore size distribution achieved by BJH method. As known, the specific surface area is evaluated by dividing the surface area by unit mass. BET study was performed to calculate the surface area, pore size and pore volume and they were found to be 80.51 m2g−1, 1.8 nm and 0.191 cm3g−1, respectively. Actually, these obtained structures lead to easing the adsorption of PEDOT:PSS fragments on the surfaces of CTe which help in improving the conducting paths that enhance the conductivity, improve the EM absorption and therefore growing the SET value [1–3].

Fig. 3 presents the typical S(2p) spectra of the films. As shown, for x = 0 wt% (Te/PEDOT:PSS), there are two bands at binding energy of 166–168 eV and 162–166 eV for the sulphur of sulfonate groups in PSS unit and thiophene groups in PEDOT unit [50]. As shown, for the CTe/PEDOT:PSS film, three observations were detected (i) the growth in the S(2p) height of PEDOT. (ii) A decline in the S(2p) height of PSS. (iii) A shift in the position of S(2p) of PSS to a lower energy, showing the impact of Cdots on the structure of the films. This shift can be accredited to the strong hydrogen H-bonding and strong π-π interactions among PEDOT:PSS (by PSS segments) and Cdots (by surface groups). As known, the PEDOT:PSS structure has a PSS polymer as bonded with PEDOT and free without bonded. These interactions among Cdots and the (bonded and/or free) PSS lead to increasing the carrier transports and enables the charge transferring channels by decreasing the carrier jump barriers, hopping, and creating greater σ values.

The above observations have been confirmed by SEM analysis (Fig. 4 ). As shown, at x = 0 wt% (Te/PEDOT:PSS), nano-Te rods are separated from the PEDOT:PSS film and located on its surface with large densities (Fig. 4a), indicating the weak interaction between PEDOT:PSS and Te nanorods which is reflecting the weak compatibility, where there is only weak van der waals forces among PEDOT:PSS polymers and nano-Te rods (Scheme 1). However at x = 2 wt%, CTe particles are fine dispersed inside the PEDOT:PSS with a high homogeneity and compatibility where a very small quantity of CTe nanoparticles are separated and distributed on the PEDOT:PSS surfaces and the largest quantity of CTe sink inside the matrix (Fig. 4b). With increasing the x concentration, a better dispersion of CTe nanoparticles in the PEDOT:PSS was obtained owing to the strong H-bonding, the strong π-π interfacial interactions among the polymeric segments and the Cdots on the surfaces of Te and the van der waals interactions among the Te and PEDOT:PSS polymers (Fig. 4c,d). Increasing the x concentration (8 wt%), a good dispersion property of the CTe in the whole PEDOT:PSS template has been observed with a good adhesion features between the PEDOT:PSS matrix and the CTe thanks to the growth in the Cdots in the polymeric template which leads to increasing the strong H-bonding and the strong π-π interfacial interaction in the polymeric template, achieving a good dispersion, good adhesion, homogeneity and high compatibility in the PEDOT:PSS template (Fig. 4e). Also, SEM (Fig. 4e) displays that with increasing the x concentrations to 8 wt%, a high packing density structure was obtained. Actually these structures lead to enhancing the particle-to-particle connectivity feature, enhancing the aspect ratio parameter, and raising σ. In fact, this structure has an advantage in accelerating the carrier mobilities, increasing the σ [40–45] and therefore raising the SET [1–3]. Importantly, the length of the Te nano-rods reduced with increasing the amount of Cdots and seems to be destroyed. This can be illustrated on the basis of the mechanism of solid-solution-solid transformation [51] which stated that the diameter and length of Te can be controlled. In a liquid media, Te can be submitted to oxidization and/or reduction reactions which affect the Te morphology (length and diameter). Such in our cases (at low x content), a small amount of Cdots adorned the Te surface which produces an increase in the contact surface between the Te terminals and the liquid (solvent of PEDOT:PSS) which allows the growth Te and keeps its morphology. However, in the case of large x content (x = 8 wt%), large amount of Cdots adorns the Te surface which in turn prevents it from contacting with the liquid and protects it from any reaction with liquid, and this leads to reducing the average length or destroying it [51]. On the other hand, at x = 10 wt% (Cdots/PEDOT:PSS), a good dispersion and high compatibility of Cdots were observed inside the matrix of the PEDOT:PSS polymer due to the strong H-bonding and the strong π-π interfacial interactions among the polymeric segments and the Cdots which lead to creating conducting paths of Cdots, improving the carrier mobilities, increasing the σ and hence increasing the SET values (Fig. 4f). In fact, These morphologies led to (i) increasing the interfacial regions in the composite e.g. (interfaces of PEDOT:PSS-Cdots, PEDOT:PSS-Te and PEDOT:PSS-CTe) which lead to increasing the interfacial polarizations inside the films, scattering of the transmitted EM wave, this produces a repeated internal EM absorption, reflections, and hence SET. (ii) Increasing the electron density inside the matrix which plays a vital role in stimulating the electric current in the matrix, which in turn produces a reduction in the EM energies in the matrix by the ohmic loss phenomena. (iii) Enhancing the connectivity feature (particle-to-particle performance), enhancing the aspect ratio parameter, improving σ and hence SET.

Return to Fig. 5a, the σ ⊥ of all shields was figured with the x concentration. As detected, the Te/PEDOT:PSS shield (x = 0 wt%) has a σ ⊥ value ~28.94 ± 5 S/cm. Alternatively, Cdots/PEDOT:PSS film (x = 10 wt%) has a σ ⊥ value ~24.75 ± 4 S/cm. However, when Cdots adorned the Te surface and became embedded into the PEDOT:PSS, the σ ⊥ value dramatically increased to be 51.40 ± 6, 101.46 ± 5, 150.36 ± 6 and 200.52 ± 4 S/cm for x = 2, 4, 6 and 8 wt%. The growth in σ ⊥ is ascribed to the formation of conductive paths from CTe in polymeric template [1–3]. As a result, the charge transfer complexs among PEDOT:PSS segments and CTe are produced due to these conductive paths which are responsible for increasing the carrier mobility, charge carrier concentrations in the films and therefore raising the σ of the films. In fact, the basic explanation for increasing the σ is attributed to the growth in the carrier concentrations (n) and/or the carrier's mobility (μ) inside the composites [40–45]. Fundamentally, the σ can be represented by the following relation:
(1)
σ = neμ

Fig. 5b shows the μ measurements as a function of x concentration. As observed, the μ values dramatically improved with the growth in the synergistic concentration and this illustrates the growth in the σ value. In fact, when Te surfaces are adorned with Cdots, this leads to increasing the charge transfer complexs among the functionalized groups on Cdots and the PEDOT:PSS segments which increases the adsorption of the polymeric segments to the CTe surfaces [28,29]. Therefore, the strong interaction has been established between the CTe nanoparticles and PEDOT:PSS segments via the strong π-π conjugated interactions and strong H-bonding. As a result, an expansion in the molecular conformation of polymer is produced and a high order of chain packing occurred by the growth of the adsorbed polymeric segments on the CTe surface. These performances produce the PEDOT:PSS ring twisting which produces a reduction in the π-π conjugated defects in the polymeric backbones, increases the degree of electron delocalization, hence reducing the barrier of the hopping carriers, and hence raising the μ values in CTe/PEDOT:PS films [52–54]. Moreover, the strong π-π interaction of CTe with the polymeric segments leads to bridging the carrier transports, which raises the carrier mobilities, and therefore growing the σ. Actually, the adornment of Cdots on the Te surfaces and strong adsorption of the polymeric molecules on the CTe surface allow the creation of CTe conductive paths in the matrix which ease the charge transfer complexs by decreasing the carrier jump barriers, increasing the conductivity and hence improving the SET [55]. More Importantly, the μ value decreased at x = 10 wt% (Cdots/PEDOT:PSS). In fact, this was attributed to (i) the strong interaction between Cdots and PEDOT:PSS layers through hydrogen bonding, π-π interfacial interactions which lead to restricting the polymeric chains from moving and (ii) the absence of nano-Te rods which leads to decreasing the number of conducting paths, raises the energy barriers of the electron transitions, causes the impede of the charge transports where the Te plays as a path (bridge) to transport the charge from Cdots – Te – PEDOT:PSS layers (via electron hopping and electron migration phenomena).

Actually the materials ability to reflect or absorb the EM waves at high frequencies is called the electromagnetic interference shielding or (EMI). Indeed, when 10 ≥ SET ≥ 30 dB, this is called the low shielding level, 30 ≥ SET ≥ 60 dB this is called the practical shielding level, 60 ≥ SET ≥ 90 dB this is called a high shielding level and when 90 ≥ SET ≥ 120 dB this is called the exceptional shielding level [2,3]. Basically, the total shielding effectiveness SET evaluates from the contribution of the total shielding effectiveness thanks to multi reflection SEM, reflection SER and absorption SEA. Actually, SET is identified by the following relation,

It is known that the contribution of (SEM) in the total SET mechanism can be ignored when SET ≥ 15 dB [56]. As a result, the SET value can be evaluated from,

The SET of the (CTe/PEDOT:PSS) composites is plotted with the x concentration in the frequency domain ƒ = 8–12 GHz (Fig. 6a). The SET of the films at x = 0 wt% (Te/PEDOT:PSS) is 38.89 dB at ƒ = 8 GHz. In fact, PEDOT:PSS polymer has a SET < 20 dB [57]. Therefore, the increment in the SET value is ascribed to the being of Te which is characterized with a high absorbing EM wave features. Also, the SET of the films at x = 10 wt% (Cdots/PEDOT:PSS) is 44.97 dB at ƒ = 8 GHz. This is ascribed to the presence of Cdots with high absorbing radicals such as their functionalized group (−OH/−COOH) and the high conductivity which allow increasing the EM absorption [1–3]. More focusing, the SET value is (~49.07 dB) when inserting x = 2 wt% inside the PEDOT:PSS matrix. Also, the SET value increases to be 54.08 dB, 58.44 dB, and 63.17 dB for 4, 6 and 8 wt%, respectively. As observed, the growth of synergistic concentration leads to increasing the SET value and the maximum SET value was found 63.17 dB at x = 8 wt%, referring that this composite has able to block 99.99999952% of the incident EM radiations which agree with the desires and requirements for military and commercial applications [58]. This is because of the synergistic effect of both Cdots and Te nanorods and the growth in the synergistic concentration inside the PEDOT:PSS matrix lead to enhancing the particle-to-particle connective property, creating a continual CTe conducting paths, increasing the conductivity and therefore enhancing the SET value [1–3,24]. In fact, the benefit of CTe with the nano-rods shape of Te produces an increase in the interacted surface areas between the nano-fillers and the incident EM waves, producing large numbers of continuous conducting paths of CTe, generating a higher mobile charge carrier's density in the matrix, more interactions with the incident EM waves and therefore growing the SET. The obtained results refer that these fabricated materials have the advantage to be a good shield in industrial applications.

In fact, the mechanism of SET can be illustrated as (Fig. 6b); (i) The embedding of CTe nanofillers inside the matrix and the usage of conducting polymeric materials lead to producing a polymeric film with high conducting surfaces, which delivers a huge quantity of free electrons on the film surfaces, therefore creating reflection of the EM radiation. (ii) CTe with a huge density of electrons plays a vital role in inducing the electric current in the shields, which in turn producing a reduction in the energies of the EM waves in the matrix by the ohmic loss phenomena [59]. (iii) Both the film structure and the interfacial polarization formed in the films are contributing in the scattering of the transmitted EM wave, and this leads to generating a number of internal EM absorption and reflections [1–3]. (iv) Inserting both Cdots and Te which are characterized with high EM absorbing features produces an increase in the EM wave's absorption and raising the SET value. (v) The embedding of CTe into PEDOT:PSS polymer produces filler/polymer interfaces in addition to the interfaces exist in the shield e.g. PEDOT:PSS-Cdots, PEDOT:PSS-Te, and PEDOT:PSS-CTe, these huge interfacial regions lead to improving the impedance mismatch [60]. (vi) Also, the growth of CTe particles in the shield produces an increase in the numbers of conducting CTe paths which make a CTe resistor-like and a PEDOT:PSS capacitor-like “capacitor-resistor phenomenon” under a great GHz frequencies. Actually, this produces an increase in the numbers of charge carriers which sequentially increasing the EM absorption in the shield and hence growing the SET. Besides that, the functional (-OH and/or -COO) groups of Cdots and PSS with high electronegativity may induce dipoles with Te which in turn interacts with the incident EM waves and produces the polarization loss which may reduce the EM energy/radiation [1–3,58]. Therefore, the SET value of the obtained material is greatly enhanced. Actually, just when the EM wave strikes the film, the incident wave will be divided into two fragments, a fragment of the incident EM waves is immediately reflected at the shield surfaces due to the large surface charge density, and the surviving EM waves will pass into the film (Fig. 6b). A part of surviving wave is absorbed by Cdots, Te, CTe nano-fillers and PEDOT:PSS segments. The other part of surviving wave passes to CTe particles which they are located close to the second film surface and divided into absorbed and transmitted waves, and the other part will be reflected by CTe creating internal reflections among the CTe fillers. These reflected waves will suffer from multi-reflection and multi-absorption modes by the CTe fillers inside the matrix, and then disappear. This physical phenomenon is repeated inside the matrix because of the being of great number of CTe fillers in the matrix. Thus, a minor portion of EM wave can pass from the film.

Moreover, Fig. 6(c) presents the relation between the transmission coefficient (T) absorption coefficient (A) and reflection coefficient (R) of the obtained shields. Actually, the ability of the shield to transmit, reflect, and/or absorb EM waves can be clearly illustrated by these three coefficients (T R, and A). As detected, the value of A dramatically increased with growing the CTe content in the shield while the value of R is decreased. Also, the value of T appeared to be small. More excitingly, the value of A is extremely higher than R. This means that the main shielding mechanism in these shields is evaluated by absorption. This is due to (i) the synergistic effect of Cdots and Te with their high EM absorbing characters. (ii) The presence of CTe particles and their good dispersion feature allow more interactions between EM waves and the functionalized groups (−OH and −COOH) on the Cdots surfaces, and create multiple scattering of EM waves which act as multiple channels for absorbing these waves. Also, the minute values of (T ~ zero) refer to the high consumption of the EM wave inside the shields.

The SER and SEA of the films are plotted with the x concentrations in (Fig. 7 ). As shown, the SEA is greater than the SER; this proves that the contribution of the absorption is larger than reflection in the shielding mechanism. As shown, the SEA is figured with x concentration (Fig. 7a). As seen SEA is 29.49 dB at 8 GHz for x = 0 wt% (Te/PEDOT:PSS). This large value indicates the high EM absorption performance of Te particles. This is attributed to (i) the Te particles create a conductive network, which produces an extra free electrons that accumulate the charges at the interfaces, and hence raising the dielectric properties that is directly responsible for absorbing and dissipate of the microwave. (ii) The conductive loss that is produced from the electrons hopping phenomenon in the Te particles. On the other hand, the SEA is 33.55 dB at 8 GHz for x = 10 wt% (Cdots/PEDOT:PSS). As seen the (Cdots/PEDOT:PSS) has a large SEA value due to the large EM absorption of Cdots nanoparticles. This is ascribed to the groups (−COOH and −OH) functionalized the Cdots surfaces. In fact, these functioning (−COOH and −OH) groups, defects and edges on the Cdots surfaces lead to creating a multiple scattering of EM waves which act as multiple channels for absorbing these waves. Besides, the tunneling electrons from CDots contribute in absorbing the EM waves. As seen, the SEA values of CTe/PEDOT:PSS film are 36.62, 39.92, 42.03, and 45.49 dB at 8 GHz for x = 2, 4, 6 and 8 wt%, respectively. This growth in SEA is attributed to (i) the growth in CTe which produces an increase in the conductivity and hence SEA (Fig. 7b). (ii) The synergistic effect of Cdots and Te with their high EM absorbing characters. (iii) The growth in synergistic concentration leads to increasing the carrier mobility which induces a current inside the films and hence increasing the ohmic loss. (iv) The large polarization loss, (v) large connectivity behavior of the CTe inside the matrix and (vi) the growth in the crosslinking densities, where the growth in synergistic concentration leads to increasing the interactions among CTe particles (via functionalized groups of Cdots) and the PEDOT:PSS polymer (via PSS) through the π-π interactions and hydrogen H-bonding interactions that enhance the crosslinking densities inside the matrix [61,62]. Certainly, huge amount of mobile charge carriers and also huge amount of the conducting networks which they are created in the CTe/PEDOT;PSS films by growing the synergistic concentrations lead to attenuating the penetrating waves. Also, the dissipation effect of charge carrier and the electric dipole relaxation processes at interfaces are contributing in the microwave absorption. Besides that, a well dispersion feature of CTe nanoparticles allows more interactions between EM waves and the functionalized groups (−OH and −COOH) on the Cdots surfaces, and hence creating the growth in EM absorption.

Alternatively, the SER is figured with the x concentration (Fig. 7c). As seen, the SER is 9.18 dB at 8 GHz for x = 0 wt% (Te/PEDOT:PSS) and the SER is 11.36 dB at 8 GHz for x = 10 wt% (Cdots/PEDOT:PSS). Also, the SER values are 12.44, 14.16, 16.40 and 17.68 dB at 8 GHz for x = 2, 4, 6 and 8 wt%, respectively. As shown, the increase in SER is attributed to the higher σ of the films. Indeed, the impacts of the σ on the EM shielding parameters were reported in various literatures [59,60]. In fact, the cause behind the increment in SER is ascribed to existence of extra surface charges thanks to the synergetic effect of Te and Cdots [1–3]. The higher dispersion property and good compatibility of CTe in the polymeric template lead to creating extra surface charges, raising the σ and therefore raising the reflections. The SER is figured with the σ// to prove the above illustration (Fig. 7d). As observed, the SER improved with growing the σ//. Actually, the growth in synergistic concentration leads to increasing the σ// which means the increase in the surface charges on the film surfaces, and hence increasing the SER value.
Dielectric Constants Study.

In fact, ε' refers to the material ability to store the energy of EM radiation from an applied electric filed E. The ε ' ' refers to the quantity of energy loss of EM radiation by the materials and its value is connected with σ. The ε' is plotted with the frequency for various concentration of (x) (Fig. 8 ). As detected, (i) the ε' spectra are independent on the frequency; but (ii) the ε' spectra are increased with the synergistic concentration and the highest value was achieved at x = 8 wt%.

As seen, the ε' improved with the increase in the synergistic concentration. This improvement is accredited to (i) the increment in the concentrations of the free charge carrier's by increasing the synergistic concentration which leads to creating a huge ε' values and hence raising the EM absorption. (ii) The increase in the interfacial polarizations between the CTe nanoparticles and the PEDOT:PSS creates a growth in the ε' value [2,3,63]. (iii) The very small particle size of the CTe nanoparticles and their large surface area lead to enhancing the space charge polarization which produces an increase in the ε' value [64]. (iv) The increase in the synergistic concentration and their small sizes produce a growth in the volume fraction of nanoparticles in the matrix of composite, and hence raising the dielectric constant of the nanocomposite according to the theory of effective medium [23]. On the other hand, ε ' ' is figured with the x concentration (Fig. 8b). As detected, (i) ε ' ' spectra are independent on the frequency, however (ii) ε ' ' spectra are significantly improved with the synergistic concentration and the highest value was achieved at x = 8 wt%. As detected, the increase in ε ' ' > 1 is attributed to the growth in the CTe in the CTe/PEDOT:PSS, which creates continuous conductive paths of CTe, raising the conductivity and hence raising the (ε ' ') according to the σ = 2πfε
o
ε ' ' [20,61–63].

Table 1 shows the comparison among the SET of the films achieved in the current study and their counterparts based on polymer nanocomposites in the frequency band 8–12 GHz. As shown, the SET value of our EM shield is greater than that of the other earlier studies such as AgNW/Cotton fabric (32.98 dB), AgNW/Nanocellulose Aerogels (~62.3 dB), Chitosan-g-PANI/epoxy (5.9 dB), CNT/Cellulose nanofiber film (46.4 dB) (Table 1), PS/Cu NW (~32.2 dB) and PES/ Carbon fiber (5.7–12.6 dB) (Table S1). In addition, our (CTe/PEDOT:PSS) films have SET smaller than that of other composites e.g. Ti3C2Tx bulk (92 dB), rGO@Fe3O4/T-ZnO/Ag/WPU (~87.2 dB) (Table 1), flexible graphite (129.4 dB) and PANI/MWCNT/graphene (85–98 dB) (Table S1). This comparison presents some merits for our films e.g. their great conductivity, great shielding parameters at tiny amounts of Cdots and Te nanorods, as well as, affordability, harmlessness, fast production, easy manufacture, bendablity, green, and the usage of a minor amount of raw materials when compared with other studies. Alternatively, our films have some drawbacks e.g. a relative small conductivity and total SET to the other polymeric nanocomposites. In summary, it can be suggested that the increment in both Cdots and Te contents and/or embedding this shield with high electrical and/or magnetic agents may rise the conductivity and hence raising the SET of the nanocomposite.

A new electromagnetic shield based on Carbon dots adorned Telluride nanorods incorporated into PEDOT:PSS (CTe/PEDOT:PSS) conducting composites has been constructed for the EMI shielding applications at the frequency regime 8–12 GHz for the first time. The impact of synergistic effect of Cdots and Te on surface morphology, electrical and electromagnetic features was studied according to the matrix (C x Te10-x ), where x is the Cdots concentration. The adornment of Cdots on the Te surface helps in achieving a high dispersion, an excellent adhesion property, and well compatibility of CTe nanoparticles in the PEDOT:PSS matrix according to TEM and SEM. The isotherm type IV, a hysteresis loop (H3-type), surface area ~ 80.51 m2g−1, pore size ~1.8 nm and pore volume ~ 0.191 cm3g−1 of CTe help in facilitating the adsorption of polymeric chains on the CTe surfaces, improving the σ, improving the EM absorptions and hence raising the SET. As a result of these features, at x = 8 wt%, the σ
// = 237.87 ± 5 S/cm and σ ⊥ = 200.52 ± 4 S/cm were achieved owing to the high packing of PEDOT nano-crystallites and the formation of conductive paths inside the shields. The highest (SET ~ 63.17 dB) was achieved at 8 GHz for x = 8 wt% which means that this composite was blocked 99.99999952% of the incident EM radiations thanks to the high σ and the synergistic effect. The absorption is the main shielding effectiveness mechanism in this composite. Finally, this study introduces a new, excellent EM wave absorber based on CTe/PEDOT:PSS shields which will have a prospective benefits in biological applications, microwave and electronic apparatuses at an affordable cost.

Sobhy S. Ibrahim: Review.
